_metadata.instruction: For each object, find the matching concept in concepts.json by 'name' (exact match, case-sensitive). Add or overwrite: thinkerBio, quote, tags, related, books. All other fields remain unchanged.
_metadata.affiliate_note: books[].url format: https://bookshop.org/a/philosophia/ISBN -- replace ISBN after joining Bookshop.org affiliate program.

completion.name: The Trolley Problem's Legacy
completion.thinkerBio: Joshua Greene (b. 1974) at Harvard developed the dual-process neuroscientific account of trolley-style moral judgment in Moral Tribes (2013); Judith Jarvis Thomson herself revisited her original cases decades later in 'The Trolley Problem Revisited.'
completion.quote: Personal moral dilemmas engage emotional response; impersonal ones engage cold deliberation -- the trolley problem reveals two different cognitive systems at work.

related.name: The Trolley Problem
related.note: The legacy traces forty years of philosophical work generated by Foot's and Thomson's original cases.

related.name: Moral Psychology
related.note: Greene's neuroscientific findings using trolley cases transformed both moral philosophy and empirical moral psychology simultaneously.

related.name: Utilitarianism
related.note: Greene's dual-process model suggests utilitarian judgment is associated with deliberative override of automatic deontological intuitions.

related.name: Categorical Imperative
related.note: The persistence of deontological intuitions in trolley cases challenges purely consequentialist or purely Kantian accounts of moral judgment.

books.title: Moral Tribes
books.author: Joshua Greene
books.why: Greene's full neuroscientific and philosophical account of what trolley cases reveal about moral psychology.

books.title: Would You Kill the Fat Man?
books.author: David Edmonds
books.why: An accessible, thorough history of the trolley problem's forty-year philosophical legacy.

books.title: Elements of Ethics
books.author: Judith Jarvis Thomson
books.why: Thomson's later reflections, including her own revisiting of the original trolley cases.

completion.name: Moral Realism vs Anti-Realism
completion.thinkerBio: Russ Shafer-Landau (b. 1963) at Wisconsin-Madison wrote the most systematic contemporary defence of moral realism; Simon Blackburn (b. 1944) represents the most sophisticated anti-realist alternative through quasi-realism.
completion.quote: The debate between moral realism and anti-realism is the central organizing question of contemporary metaethics.

related.name: Moral Realism
related.note: This entry surveys the full landscape of which moral realism is one pole.

related.name: Expressivism
related.note: Expressivism is the leading anti-realist alternative, holding moral claims express attitudes rather than state facts.

related.name: Error Theory
related.note: Error theory is the other major anti-realist position, holding moral claims aspire to truth but uniformly fail.

related.name: Metaethics: The Terrain
related.note: Both entries map the same metaethical landscape from complementary angles.

books.title: Moral Realism: A Defence
books.author: Russ Shafer-Landau
books.why: The most comprehensive contemporary defence of non-naturalist moral realism.

books.title: Spreading the Word
books.author: Simon Blackburn
books.why: The leading anti-realist alternative -- quasi-realism's attempt to earn moral objectivity without realist metaphysics.

books.title: Ethical Theory: An Anthology
books.author: Russ Shafer-Landau (ed.)
books.why: Comprehensive anthology covering both sides of the realism debate alongside normative ethics.

completion.name: Substance and Accident
completion.thinkerBio: Aristotle (384-322 BCE) developed the substance-accident distinction in the Categories and Metaphysics; it became central to medieval Scholastic philosophy and remains foundational for debates about the nature of objects and properties.
completion.quote: Substance is that which is not predicated of anything else, but of which everything else is predicated.

related.name: The Problem of Universals
related.note: The substance-accident distinction underlies debates about universals -- accidents are the properties substances instantiate.

related.name: Essentialism
related.note: Essential properties are those a substance cannot lose without ceasing to exist; accidental properties can change without destroying identity.

related.name: Identity Over Time
related.note: A substance persisting through accidental change while remaining numerically the same thing is the classical model of identity over time.

related.name: Process Philosophy
related.note: Whitehead's process philosophy denies that substance is the fundamental category -- events and processes replace substances as the basic units of reality.

books.title: Categories
books.author: Aristotle
books.why: The primary source -- Aristotle's original taxonomy of substance and the nine categories of accident.

books.title: Aristotle's Metaphysics
books.author: Aristotle (trans. Hugh Lawson-Tancred)
books.why: Aristotle's mature development of substance theory in Metaphysics Books Z, H, and Theta.

books.title: Substance: Its Nature and Existence
books.author: Joshua Hoffman and Gary Rosenkrantz
books.why: The most thorough contemporary analytic treatment of substance ontology.

completion.name: Bundle Theory
completion.thinkerBio: David Hume (1711-1776) developed bundle theory of the self in A Treatise of Human Nature; contemporary bundle theorists of objects more generally include Russell and, in a qualified form, Rudolf Carnap.
completion.quote: I never can catch myself at any time without a perception, and never can observe anything but the perception.

related.name: Substance and Accident
related.note: Bundle theory denies that objects are substances bearing properties -- they are nothing but collections of co-located properties or perceptions.

related.name: Anatta
related.note: Hume's bundle theory of the self and Buddhist anatta both deny a persistent subject underlying the flow of experience.

related.name: Personal Identity
related.note: Hume's bundle theory denies any underlying self that could ground personal identity -- there is only a constantly changing bundle of perceptions.

related.name: The Problem of Universals
related.note: Bundle theory of objects treats things as bundles of universals or tropes co-located in space, without a substratum to bear them.

books.title: A Treatise of Human Nature
books.author: David Hume
books.why: Book I, Part IV contains Hume's bundle theory of the self -- one of philosophy's most radical claims about personal identity.

books.title: Metaphysics: A Contemporary Introduction
books.author: Michael Loux
books.why: Loux's textbook gives the clearest contemporary statement of bundle theory and its problems.

books.title: Hume's Philosophy of the Self
books.author: Terence Penelhum
books.why: A scholarly study focused specifically on Hume's bundle theory and its puzzles.

completion.name: The Problem of Universals
completion.thinkerBio: Plato (428-348 BCE) defended a realist theory of Forms as universals existing independently of particulars; D.M. Armstrong (1926-2014) at Sydney developed the most influential contemporary realist alternative in A World of States of Affairs (1997).
completion.quote: A universal is something that can be wholly present in many different places at the same time.

related.name: Essentialism
related.note: Essentialism about natural kinds requires that kind-properties are real universals, not merely nominal groupings imposed by classification.

related.name: Substance and Accident
related.note: The substance-accident distinction presupposes some account of universals -- accidents are typically taken to be universal properties instantiated in particular substances.

related.name: Occam's Razor
related.note: Nominalism about universals is motivated by Occam's Razor -- universals are abstract entities that should be eliminated if particulars and resemblance can do the explanatory work instead.

related.name: Laws of Nature
related.note: Armstrong's necessitarian account of laws of nature treats them as relations between universals, giving universals a central role in explaining lawhood.

books.title: A World of States of Affairs
books.author: D.M. Armstrong
books.why: Armstrong's mature systematic metaphysics grounded in immanent realism about universals.

books.title: Universals: An Opinionated Introduction
books.author: D.M. Armstrong
books.why: The clearest short introduction to the problem of universals and its major solutions.

books.title: The Problem of Universals in Contemporary Philosophy
books.author: Gabriele Galluzzo and Michael Loux (eds.)
books.why: An anthology of contemporary essays on universals, nominalism, and trope theory.

completion.name: Mereology
completion.thinkerBio: Stanislaw Lesniewski (1886-1939) founded formal mereology in the 1910s; David Lewis (1941-2001) developed mereological universalism -- the view that any collection of objects composes a sum -- as part of his comprehensive metaphysical system.
completion.quote: Composition as identity: the parts, taken together, just are the whole.

related.name: The Ship of Theseus
related.note: The Ship of Theseus raises mereological questions about whether the identity of a whole is determined by its parts or by formal and functional structure.

related.name: Material Constitution
related.note: Material constitution puzzles directly engage mereology -- what makes the statue and the lump of clay distinct objects despite sharing all the same parts.

related.name: Identity Over Time
related.note: Mereological questions about composition bear directly on whether an object with replaced parts remains numerically identical.

related.name: Process Philosophy
related.note: Process philosophy denies that wholes are simply composed of static parts -- events and processes do not decompose mereologically in the same way as substances.

books.title: Parts of Classes
books.author: David Lewis
books.why: Lewis's systematic development of mereology and composition as identity.

books.title: Material Beings
books.author: Peter van Inwagen
books.why: Van Inwagen's restricted composition -- only living organisms compose genuine wholes -- the most influential alternative to mereological universalism.

books.title: Composition as Identity
books.author: Donald Baxter and Aaron Cotnoir (eds.)
books.why: An anthology of contemporary essays on the relationship between composition and identity.

completion.name: Grounding and Metaphysical Dependence
completion.thinkerBio: Kit Fine (b. 1946) at NYU and Jonathan Schaffer (b. 1970) at Rutgers are the leading developers of contemporary grounding theory, which seeks to formalize the relation of metaphysical dependence underlying much philosophical explanation.
completion.quote: Grounding is the relation that holds between a fact and the more fundamental facts that explain it.

related.name: Substance and Accident
related.note: Grounding theory provides a contemporary vocabulary for the relation between substances and their accidents -- accidents are grounded in the substances that bear them.

related.name: Emergence and Supervenience
related.note: Grounding is often invoked to explain supervenience relations -- mental facts may supervene on and be grounded in physical facts.

related.name: Naturalism
related.note: Naturalism is often expressed as the claim that all facts are ultimately grounded in physical facts.

related.name: Process Philosophy
related.note: Grounding theory typically assumes a layered, hierarchical structure of reality; process philosophy denies any such fixed hierarchical structure of fundamentality.

books.title: Writing the Book of the World
books.author: Theodore Sider
books.why: Sider's structuralist metaphysics develops fundamentality and grounding into a comprehensive metaphysical picture.

books.title: Metaphysical Grounding
books.author: Fabrice Correia and Benjamin Schnieder (eds.)
books.why: The essential anthology collecting the foundational papers in contemporary grounding theory.

books.title: On What Grounds What
books.author: Jonathan Schaffer
books.why: Schaffer's foundational paper developing grounding as the central relation of metaphysical structure.

completion.name: The Mind-Body Problem
completion.thinkerBio: Rene Descartes (1596-1650) gave the mind-body problem its canonical modern formulation through substance dualism; the problem has generated every major position in contemporary philosophy of mind from physicalism to panpsychism.
completion.quote: How can an unextended thinking substance causally interact with an extended physical body?

related.name: Substance Dualism
related.note: Substance dualism is Descartes's original answer to the mind-body problem -- mind and body are two distinct kinds of substance.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Functionalism is a contemporary physicalist answer -- mental states are functional roles realized by physical states, dissolving the interaction problem.

related.name: Panpsychism
related.note: Panpsychism offers another answer -- consciousness is fundamental and physical, avoiding both Cartesian dualism and physicalist reduction.

related.name: Hard Problem of Consciousness
related.note: The hard problem is the contemporary articulation of why the mind-body problem resists straightforward physicalist resolution.

books.title: Meditations on First Philosophy
books.author: Rene Descartes
books.why: The text that gave the mind-body problem its canonical modern formulation.

books.title: Philosophy of Mind
books.author: Jaegwon Kim
books.why: The clearest comprehensive textbook surveying every major position on the mind-body problem.

books.title: The Mind-Body Problem
books.author: Richard Warner and Tadeusz Szubka (eds.)
books.why: An anthology of classic and contemporary papers spanning the full range of positions.

completion.name: Reliabilism
completion.thinkerBio: Alvin Goldman (b. 1938) at Rutgers introduced process reliabilism in 'A Causal Theory of Knowing' (1967) and developed it fully in subsequent decades; he remains the most influential figure in externalist epistemology.
completion.quote: S knows that p if S's belief that p was produced by a reliable cognitive process.

related.name: The Gettier Problem
related.note: Reliabilism was developed partly to solve the Gettier problem -- reliability, not mere justification, is the additional condition required for knowledge.

related.name: Virtue Epistemology
related.note: Virtue epistemology can be understood as a refinement of reliabilism -- knowledge requires reliable processes that are also exercises of the agent's intellectual virtue.

related.name: Foundationalism
related.note: Reliabilism is externalist about justification, unlike traditional internalist foundationalism, which requires the believer to have access to their justifying reasons.

related.name: Naturalized Epistemology
related.note: Both reliabilism and naturalized epistemology ground epistemic norms in facts about cognitive processes studied by empirical psychology.

books.title: Epistemology and Cognition
books.author: Alvin Goldman
books.why: Goldman's mature systematic statement of reliabilism integrated with cognitive science.

books.title: Liaisons: Philosophy Meets the Cognitive and Social Sciences
books.author: Alvin Goldman
books.why: Goldman's later essays extending reliabilist epistemology into social epistemology.

books.title: Knowledge in a Social World
books.author: Alvin Goldman
books.why: Goldman's extension of reliabilist epistemology to social and political contexts.

completion.name: Foundationalism
completion.thinkerBio: Rene Descartes (1596-1650) is the paradigmatic classical foundationalist; Roderick Chisholm (1916-1999) at Brown developed the most systematic contemporary foundationalist epistemology across multiple editions of Theory of Knowledge.
completion.quote: Knowledge must rest on a foundation of basic beliefs that are justified without depending on any other beliefs.

related.name: Cogito Ergo Sum
related.note: The cogito is the paradigmatic foundationalist basic belief -- certain, self-evident, and the starting point for all further knowledge.

related.name: Coherentism
related.note: Foundationalism and coherentism are the two great rival structures of justification -- hierarchical foundations versus a holistic web of mutual support.

related.name: Reliabilism
related.note: Foundationalism is typically internalist, requiring the believer's access to their justifying basic beliefs; reliabilism is externalist about justification.

related.name: Skepticism
related.note: Foundationalism is motivated by the demand to escape the skeptical regress of justification by finding a non-inferentially justified stopping point.

books.title: Theory of Knowledge
books.author: Roderick Chisholm
books.why: The most systematic contemporary defence of foundationalist epistemology across three editions.

books.title: Meditations on First Philosophy
books.author: Rene Descartes
books.why: The classical foundationalist text -- finding the cogito as a certain foundation for rebuilding knowledge.

books.title: The Structure of Empirical Knowledge
books.author: Laurence BonJour
books.why: BonJour's critique of foundationalism and defence of coherentism -- essential for understanding the debate's stakes.

completion.name: Virtue Epistemology
completion.thinkerBio: Ernest Sosa (b. 1940) at Rutgers and Linda Zagzebski (b. 1946) at Oklahoma developed virtue epistemology as a systematic alternative grounding knowledge in the exercise of intellectual virtues rather than mere reliable processes or internalist justification.
completion.quote: Knowledge is apt belief -- true belief that manifests the cognitive virtue of the believer.

related.name: The Gettier Problem
related.note: Virtue epistemology responds to Gettier by requiring that knowledge succeed through the exercise of intellectual virtue, blocking lucky true belief.

related.name: Reliabilism
related.note: Virtue epistemology can be seen as a refinement of reliabilism, identifying the relevant reliable process with the exercise of an agent's specific intellectual virtue.

related.name: Virtue Ethics
related.note: Virtue epistemology applies the structure of Aristotelian virtue ethics to the theory of knowledge -- character traits, not rules, ground epistemic excellence.

related.name: Epistemic Humility
related.note: Intellectual humility is one of the central virtues identified by virtue epistemologists, alongside open-mindedness and intellectual courage.

books.title: A Virtue Epistemology
books.author: Ernest Sosa
books.why: Sosa's systematic development of apt belief and the AAA structure of epistemic evaluation.

books.title: Virtues of the Mind
books.author: Linda Zagzebski
books.why: Zagzebski's foundational text grounding virtue epistemology in an Aristotelian framework of intellectual virtue.

books.title: Intellectual Virtues
books.author: Jason Baehr
books.why: Baehr's accessible introduction connecting virtue epistemology to education and public discourse.

completion.name: Testimony
completion.thinkerBio: David Hume (1711-1776) gave the classic reductionist treatment of testimony in An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding; C.A.J. Coady (b. 1936) defended anti-reductionism in Testimony: A Philosophical Study (1992), arguing testimony is a basic, irreducible source of knowledge.
completion.quote: Testimony is the default-and-challenge structure of human knowledge transmission -- we believe what others tell us unless given specific reason for doubt.

related.name: Epistemic Injustice
related.note: Testimonial injustice is the systematic deflation of a speaker's credibility based on social identity -- a central application of testimony theory.

related.name: Standpoint Epistemology
related.note: Both testimony theory and standpoint epistemology examine how social position affects the reception and credibility of knowledge claims.

related.name: Social Epistemology
related.note: Testimony is the paradigm case studied by social epistemology -- most human knowledge is acquired through trusting others rather than direct observation.

related.name: Skepticism
related.note: Hume's reductionist account of testimony treats it skeptically -- testimonial belief requires independent inductive evidence for the reliability of the source.

books.title: Testimony: A Philosophical Study
books.author: C.A.J. Coady
books.why: The foundational contemporary text defending anti-reductionism about testimonial knowledge.

books.title: Epistemic Injustice
books.author: Miranda Fricker
books.why: Fricker's extension of testimony theory to the ethics of credibility and social power.

books.title: An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding
books.author: David Hume
books.why: Hume's classical reductionist account of testimony, requiring independent evidence of source reliability.

completion.name: The Problem of Induction
completion.thinkerBio: David Hume (1711-1776) posed the problem of induction in A Treatise of Human Nature (1739); Nelson Goodman (1906-1998) deepened it dramatically with the new riddle of induction in Fact, Fiction, and Forecast (1955).
completion.quote: Past experience can be allowed to give direct and certain information of those precise objects only, and that precise period of time which fell under its cognizance.

related.name: Causation
related.note: Hume's problem of induction and his analysis of causation are two sides of the same skeptical insight about necessary connection.

related.name: The Grue Paradox
related.note: Goodman's grue paradox shows the problem of induction runs deeper than Hume supposed -- we also need a theory of which predicates are projectible.

related.name: Falsifiability
related.note: Popper's falsificationism was partly designed to avoid relying on induction altogether, replacing confirmation with conjecture and refutation.

related.name: Bayesian Epistemology
related.note: Bayesian confirmation theory attempts to give a probabilistic, non-Humean account of how evidence supports inductive generalizations.

books.title: An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding
books.author: David Hume
books.why: Hume's original and most accessible statement of the problem of induction.

books.title: Fact, Fiction, and Forecast
books.author: Nelson Goodman
books.why: Goodman's new riddle of induction -- the grue paradox that deepened Hume's original problem.

books.title: The Problem of Induction and Its Solution
books.author: Wesley Salmon
books.why: Salmon's survey of attempted solutions to the problem of induction across the twentieth century.

completion.name: Social Epistemology
completion.thinkerBio: Alvin Goldman (b. 1938) founded contemporary social epistemology with Knowledge in a Social World (1999); Helen Longino (b. 1944) developed a parallel feminist social epistemology of science emphasizing communal critical norms.
completion.quote: Most of what any individual knows, they know because they trust others, not because they verified it themselves.

related.name: Testimony
related.note: Testimony is the central case studied by social epistemology -- the social transmission of belief through trust rather than direct observation.

related.name: Standpoint Epistemology
related.note: Both fields examine how social structures and power relations shape the production and distribution of knowledge.

related.name: Epistemic Injustice
related.note: Epistemic injustice extends social epistemology to the fair distribution of epistemic credibility and conceptual resources.

related.name: Paradigm Shift
related.note: Kuhn's account of paradigms as social phenomena anticipates social epistemology's emphasis on community structure in knowledge production.

books.title: Knowledge in a Social World
books.author: Alvin Goldman
books.why: The foundational systematic text of contemporary social epistemology.

books.title: The Fate of Knowledge
books.author: Helen Longino
books.why: Longino's social epistemology of science -- objectivity as a property of communities, not individuals.

books.title: Social Epistemology: Essential Readings
books.author: Alvin Goldman and Dennis Whitcomb (eds.)
books.why: The essential anthology covering the full range of contemporary social epistemology.

completion.name: Existential Phenomenology
completion.thinkerBio: Maurice Merleau-Ponty (1908-1961) and Jean-Paul Sartre (1905-1980) jointly founded existential phenomenology by applying Husserl's phenomenological method to questions of embodied existence, freedom, and concrete situatedness.
completion.quote: Existence precedes essence, but existence is always embodied, situated, and lived from the inside.

related.name: Phenomenology of Perception
related.note: Merleau-Ponty's Phenomenology of Perception is the foundational text applying phenomenological method to embodied existence.

related.name: Existence Precedes Essence
related.note: Sartre's existentialist slogan is itself a product of applying phenomenological method to the structures of human freedom.

related.name: Embodied Cognition
related.note: Contemporary embodied cognition science draws directly on Merleau-Ponty's existential phenomenological account of the lived body.

related.name: Intersubjectivity
related.note: Existential phenomenology grounds intersubjectivity in embodied encounter rather than abstract theoretical inference about other minds.

books.title: Phenomenology of Perception
books.author: Maurice Merleau-Ponty
books.why: The foundational text of existential phenomenology -- the body as the lived, situated subject of perception and action.

books.title: Being and Nothingness
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: Sartre's existential phenomenology of freedom, the Other, and concrete situated consciousness.

books.title: The Phenomenology Reader
books.author: Dermot Moran and Tim Mooney (eds.)
books.why: Comprehensive anthology covering Husserl, Heidegger, Sartre, and Merleau-Ponty in the development of phenomenology.

completion.name: Absurdism and Revolt
completion.thinkerBio: Albert Camus (1913-1960) developed absurdism in The Myth of Sisyphus (1942) and extended its political dimension in The Rebel (1951); his sustained public dispute with Sartre over the latter's communism stemmed partly from this political development.
completion.quote: There is no fate that cannot be surmounted by scorn -- one must imagine Sisyphus happy.

related.name: The Absurd
related.note: The Myth of Sisyphus establishes the absurd as the fundamental confrontation between human need for meaning and the universe's silence.

related.name: Nihilism
related.note: Camus's revolt is explicitly an alternative to both nihilistic resignation and the false consolations of religious or political absolutism.

related.name: Civil Disobedience
related.note: Camus's political revolt and civil disobedience traditions both ground resistance to injustice in individual moral conviction rather than historical inevitability.

related.name: Existence Precedes Essence
related.note: Both Camus and Sartre reject pre-given meaning, but Camus rejects Sartre's claim that radical freedom can fully resolve the absurd.

books.title: The Myth of Sisyphus
books.author: Albert Camus
books.why: The foundational text of absurdism -- Camus's argument that Sisyphus must be imagined happy.

books.title: The Rebel
books.author: Albert Camus
books.why: Camus's political extension of absurdism into a philosophy of revolt and resistance to totalitarianism.

books.title: Camus: A Critical Examination
books.author: David Sprintzen
books.why: A thorough philosophical examination of Camus's development from the absurd to revolt.

completion.name: Time and Existence
completion.thinkerBio: Henri Bergson (1859-1941) developed the philosophy of duration in Time and Free Will (1889); Martin Heidegger (1889-1976) radicalized the analysis of temporality as the fundamental structure of Dasein's existence in Being and Time (1927).
completion.quote: Duration is the continuous progress of the past which gnaws into the future and which swells as it advances.

related.name: Being-toward-death
related.note: Heidegger's analysis of authentic temporality culminates in being-toward-death -- the unity of past, present, and future as Dasein projects toward its end.

related.name: Thrownness
related.note: Thrownness and temporality together constitute finite Dasein -- thrown from a past it did not choose, projecting toward a future it does not control.

related.name: Eternal Recurrence
related.note: Both Heidegger's existential temporality and Nietzsche's eternal recurrence use the structure of time to reveal what matters in how one lives.

related.name: The Reality of Time
related.note: Bergson's lived duration and the physics of time debate (A-theory vs B-theory) both ask whether temporal passage is objectively real or merely subjective.

books.title: Time and Free Will
books.author: Henri Bergson
books.why: Bergson's foundational distinction between lived duration and spatialized, scientific time.

books.title: Being and Time
books.author: Martin Heidegger
books.why: Heidegger's systematic analysis of temporality as the fundamental structure of Dasein's existence.

books.title: A Companion to Heidegger
books.author: Hubert Dreyfus and Mark Wrathall (eds.)
books.why: Comprehensive scholarly essays on Heidegger's philosophy including his analysis of temporality.

completion.name: Freedom and Responsibility
completion.thinkerBio: Jean-Paul Sartre (1905-1980) developed the existentialist account of radical freedom and total responsibility in Being and Nothingness (1943); Simone de Beauvoir (1908-1986) refined it with the concept of situated freedom in The Ethics of Ambiguity (1947).
completion.quote: We are condemned to be free, and with this freedom comes total responsibility for what we make of ourselves.

related.name: Existence Precedes Essence
related.note: Radical freedom and total responsibility follow directly from the claim that existence precedes essence -- there is no fixed nature to excuse our choices.

related.name: Bad Faith
related.note: Bad faith is the flight from the responsibility that radical freedom entails -- the comforting fiction that one's choices were determined by external factors.

related.name: Situation
related.note: De Beauvoir's concept of situation refines Sartrean freedom -- freedom is always exercised within and against material and social constraints, not in an abstract vacuum.

related.name: Free Will vs Determinism
related.note: Existentialist freedom and the analytic free will debate both ask whether and how genuine agency is possible, though existentialism emphasizes lived experience over causal metaphysics.

books.title: Being and Nothingness
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: Sartre's full ontological account of radical freedom and the responsibility it entails.

books.title: The Ethics of Ambiguity
books.author: Simone de Beauvoir
books.why: De Beauvoir's refinement of Sartrean freedom through the concept of situation and material constraint.

books.title: Existentialism Is a Humanism
books.author: Jean-Paul Sartre
books.why: Sartre's most accessible defence of existentialist freedom against charges of moral relativism and despair.

completion.name: Death and Dying
completion.thinkerBio: Epicurus (341-270 BCE) developed the symmetry argument that death cannot harm the one who dies; Martin Heidegger (1889-1976) and Bernard Williams (1929-2003) developed the most influential modern challenges to Epicurean consolation.
completion.quote: Death, the most terrifying of ills, is nothing to us, since so long as we exist, death is not with us; but when death comes, then we do not exist.

related.name: Being-toward-death
related.note: Heidegger's analysis of being-toward-death directly challenges Epicurean consolation -- death matters because it structures the meaning of one's whole existence.

related.name: The Absurd
related.note: Camus's confrontation with the absurd is partly a confrontation with mortality and the question of whether life's brevity undermines its meaning.

related.name: Consciousness and Death
related.note: Both entries address the philosophical significance of mortality, one through existentialist phenomenology and one through personal identity theory.

related.name: Eudaimonia
related.note: Aristotle's eudaimonia requires a complete life -- death's timing partly determines whether a life achieved genuine flourishing.

books.title: Letter to Menoeceus
books.author: Epicurus
books.why: Epicurus's original symmetry argument for why death should not be feared.

books.title: Mortal Questions
books.author: Thomas Nagel
books.why: Nagel's influential challenge to Epicurean consolation -- why death can genuinely be bad for the one who dies.

books.title: Being and Time
books.author: Martin Heidegger
books.why: Heidegger's existential analysis of being-toward-death as constitutive of authentic existence.

completion.name: Inauthenticity and the They-Self
completion.thinkerBio: Martin Heidegger (1889-1976) introduced das Man (the they-self) and inauthenticity in Being and Time (1927) as the default, anonymous mode of everyday existence from which authentic existence must be actively won.
completion.quote: Everyone is the other, and no one is himself -- the they-self, which everyday Dasein is, is the nobody.

related.name: Authenticity
related.note: The they-self and inauthenticity are the default conditions from which authentic existence must be won through confrontation with one's finitude and freedom.

related.name: Bad Faith
related.note: Sartre's bad faith and Heidegger's inauthenticity are parallel analyses of flight from genuine selfhood, grounded in different ontologies.

related.name: Thrownness
related.note: The they-self is one way of being thrown into a pre-given social world of inherited interpretations, practices, and norms.

related.name: Hegemony
related.note: Both the they-self and Gramscian hegemony describe how conformity to dominant social patterns operates without explicit coercion.

books.title: Being and Time
books.author: Martin Heidegger
books.why: The source -- Division One develops das Man and inauthenticity as fundamental existential structures.

books.title: Heidegger: A Very Short Introduction
books.author: Michael Inwood
books.why: The most accessible introduction to Heidegger's account of the they-self and inauthenticity.

books.title: The Present Age
books.author: Soren Kierkegaard
books.why: Kierkegaard's earlier critique of conformity and the public anticipates Heidegger's they-self by nearly a century.

completion.name: Frege's Sense and Reference
completion.thinkerBio: Gottlob Frege (1848-1925) introduced the sense-reference distinction in 'On Sense and Reference' (1892), one of the most influential papers in the history of philosophy of language and the founding text of analytic philosophy of language.
completion.quote: The reference of a sign is the object it designates; the sense is the manner in which the object is presented.

related.name: Reference and Rigid Designation
related.note: Kripke's rigid designation theory directly challenges Frege's descriptivism -- names refer without the mediation of sense or description.

related.name: Propositional Attitudes
related.note: Frege used the sense-reference distinction to explain the opacity of propositional attitude contexts -- substituting co-referring terms can change truth value.

related.name: The Liar's Paradox
related.note: Both Frege's logical innovations and the analysis of the Liar's Paradox were part of the same late nineteenth and early twentieth century project of formalizing logic and language.

related.name: Names and Descriptions
related.note: The Frege-Russell descriptivist tradition holds that names express senses equivalent to definite descriptions, a view Kripke later challenged.

books.title: The Frege Reader
books.author: Michael Beaney (ed.)
books.why: The essential anthology of Frege's writings including the foundational sense-reference paper.

books.title: Frege: Philosophy of Language
books.author: Michael Dummett
books.why: Dummett's monumental scholarly study of Frege's philosophy of language.

books.title: Naming and Necessity
books.author: Saul Kripke
books.why: Kripke's direct challenge to the Frege-Russell descriptivist tradition -- essential for understanding the contemporary stakes.

completion.name: Verificationism
completion.thinkerBio: The logical positivists of the Vienna Circle -- including Moritz Schlick (1882-1936) and Rudolf Carnap (1891-1970) -- developed verificationism as the criterion of cognitive meaningfulness; A.J. Ayer (1910-1989) popularized it in English in Language, Truth and Logic (1936).
completion.quote: A statement is meaningful if and only if it is either analytically true or empirically verifiable.

related.name: Falsifiability
related.note: Popper proposed falsifiability as an alternative criterion of meaningfulness and scientific status, rejecting verificationism's positive confirmation requirement.

related.name: Non-Cognitivism
related.note: Ayer used verificationism to argue that moral statements are not empirically verifiable and therefore not cognitively meaningful, grounding his emotivism.

related.name: The Liar's Paradox
related.note: Verificationism and the analysis of paradoxes were both part of the broader early twentieth century project of clarifying the logical structure of meaningful language.

related.name: Logical Positivism
related.note: Verificationism is the central doctrine of logical positivism, distinguishing meaningful scientific statements from meaningless metaphysical ones.

books.title: Language, Truth and Logic
books.author: A.J. Ayer
books.why: The most accessible and influential English-language statement of verificationism.

books.title: The Logical Structure of the World
books.author: Rudolf Carnap
books.why: Carnap's systematic attempt to construct all meaningful knowledge from verifiable experiential statements.

books.title: The Rise of Scientific Philosophy
books.author: Hans Reichenbach
books.why: A clear historical and philosophical account of logical positivism and verificationism's place in twentieth-century philosophy.

completion.name: Conventionalism
completion.thinkerBio: Henri Poincare (1854-1912) developed geometric conventionalism in Science and Hypothesis (1902); Rudolf Carnap (1891-1970) extended conventionalism to the choice of logical and linguistic frameworks more generally.
completion.quote: One geometry cannot be more true than another; it can only be more convenient.

related.name: Underdetermination
related.note: Conventionalism is a response to underdetermination -- when theory choice is not fixed by evidence alone, convention fills the gap.

related.name: Analytic / Synthetic Distinction
related.note: Carnap's conventionalism about linguistic frameworks depends on a stable analytic-synthetic distinction, which Quine's holism later challenged.

related.name: Scientific Realism
related.note: Conventionalism challenges scientific realism's claim that theory choice tracks objective truth rather than merely convenient stipulation.

related.name: Naturalized Epistemology
related.note: Quine's naturalized epistemology, following his rejection of the analytic-synthetic distinction, undermined the sharp conventionalist picture of framework choice.

books.title: Science and Hypothesis
books.author: Henri Poincare
books.why: Poincare's foundational statement of geometric conventionalism.

books.title: The Logical Syntax of Language
books.author: Rudolf Carnap
books.why: Carnap's systematic extension of conventionalism to logical and linguistic framework choice.

books.title: Word and Object
books.author: W.V.O. Quine
books.why: Quine's challenge to the analytic-synthetic distinction that conventionalism in Carnap's sense depends upon.

completion.name: Anarchism
completion.thinkerBio: Pierre-Joseph Proudhon (1809-1865) coined the term in What Is Property? (1840); Mikhail Bakunin (1814-1876) and Peter Kropotkin (1842-1921) developed collectivist and communist anarchism respectively, grounding political order in voluntary cooperation rather than state coercion.
completion.quote: Property is theft -- and the state, which protects property, is the instrument of that theft.

related.name: Social Contract
related.note: Anarchism denies that any social contract could legitimately authorize a coercive state -- voluntary agreement grounds only voluntary cooperation.

related.name: Civil Disobedience
related.note: Anarchism and civil disobedience share the rejection of unjust positive law, though they differ on whether reform or abolition of the state is the ultimate goal.

related.name: Hegemony
related.note: Anarchism's vision of voluntary cooperation rejects Gramsci's analysis of how hegemonic culture naturalizes the necessity of state and class authority.

related.name: Power and Domination
related.note: Anarchist political theory provides the most radical critique of domination, extending the analysis to state authority itself, not merely particular unjust laws.

books.title: What Is Property?
books.author: Pierre-Joseph Proudhon
books.why: The foundational anarchist text -- Proudhon's argument that property is theft and the critique of coercive authority.

books.title: Mutual Aid: A Factor of Evolution
books.author: Peter Kropotkin
books.why: Kropotkin's natural-historical argument for cooperation rather than competition as the primary evolutionary and social mechanism.

books.title: Statism and Anarchy
books.author: Mikhail Bakunin
books.why: Bakunin's collectivist anarchism and his famous dispute with Marx over the role of the state in revolutionary transition.

completion.name: Socialism
completion.thinkerBio: Karl Marx (1818-1883) offered the most influential theoretical framework for socialism through his analysis of surplus value and exploitation; Robert Owen (1771-1858) and Charles Fourier (1772-1837) developed earlier utopian socialist visions of cooperative community.
completion.quote: From each according to his ability, to each according to his needs.

related.name: Alienation
related.note: Marx's critique of alienation under capitalism is the foundation of his argument that socialism would supersede capitalism's structural contradictions.

related.name: Dialectics
related.note: Marx's materialist dialectic explains socialism as the historical successor to capitalism, arising from capitalism's internal contradictions.

related.name: Justice and Equality
related.note: Socialist theories of justice extend egalitarianism to argue that structural features of capitalist property relations are themselves sources of injustice.

related.name: Hegemony
related.note: Gramsci's hegemony explains why socialist revolution did not occur as predicted -- capitalist culture maintains consent that mere economic analysis could not anticipate.

books.title: Capital: Volume 1
books.author: Karl Marx
books.why: Marx's foundational analysis of capitalist exploitation through the theory of surplus value.

books.title: The Communist Manifesto
books.author: Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels
books.why: The most accessible and historically consequential statement of socialist political theory.

books.title: Socialism: Utopian and Scientific
books.author: Friedrich Engels
books.why: Engels's account of the development from utopian socialism (Owen, Fourier) to Marxist scientific socialism.

completion.name: Justice as Fairness
completion.thinkerBio: John Rawls (1921-2002) developed justice as fairness across A Theory of Justice (1971), Political Liberalism (1993), and Justice as Fairness: A Restatement (2001), refining the theory in response to decades of philosophical critique.
completion.quote: Each person is to have an equal right to the most extensive basic liberty compatible with a like liberty for others.

related.name: Original Position
related.note: Justice as fairness is the theory that the original position thought experiment is designed to derive -- the two principles of justice.

related.name: Utilitarianism
related.note: Justice as fairness is explicitly designed as an alternative to utilitarianism, taking the separateness of persons seriously rather than aggregating welfare.

related.name: Luck Egalitarianism
related.note: Luck egalitarianism refines and radicalizes the difference principle's treatment of natural talent and social background as morally arbitrary.

related.name: Communitarianism
related.note: Communitarian critics like Sandel argue justice as fairness presupposes an impoverished, unencumbered conception of the self.

books.title: A Theory of Justice
books.author: John Rawls
books.why: The original and most comprehensive statement of justice as fairness.

books.title: Justice as Fairness: A Restatement
books.author: John Rawls
books.why: Rawls's own simplified and refined restatement, responding to decades of critique.

books.title: Political Liberalism
books.author: John Rawls
books.why: Rawls's reformulation of justice as fairness as a political rather than comprehensive moral doctrine.

completion.name: Communitarianism
completion.thinkerBio: Alasdair MacIntyre (b. 1929) diagnosed the failure of liberal moral philosophy in After Virtue (1981); Michael Sandel (b. 1953) at Harvard developed the most influential communitarian critique of Rawlsian liberalism in Liberalism and the Limits of Justice (1982).
completion.quote: The self is not prior to its ends but is constituted by the community and tradition within which it finds itself.

related.name: Liberalism
related.note: Communitarianism directly challenges liberal individualism -- the self is constituted by community, not prior to it and independent of it.

related.name: Original Position
related.note: Sandel's critique targets the unencumbered self behind the veil of ignorance as a psychologically and politically implausible abstraction.

related.name: Virtue Ethics
related.note: MacIntyre's communitarianism is grounded in his revival of Aristotelian virtue ethics, which requires shared practices and traditions for moral intelligibility.

related.name: African Communalism
related.note: Western communitarianism and African communalism both challenge liberal individualism, though African communalism makes a deeper ontological claim about community's constitutive role.

books.title: After Virtue
books.author: Alasdair MacIntyre
books.why: MacIntyre's diagnosis of modern moral philosophy's failure and his case for tradition-constituted virtue ethics.

books.title: Liberalism and the Limits of Justice
books.author: Michael Sandel
books.why: Sandel's foundational communitarian critique of Rawlsian liberal individualism.

books.title: Spheres of Justice
books.author: Michael Walzer
books.why: Walzer's pluralist communitarianism -- justice as relative to the social meanings of different goods within particular communities.

completion.name: Feminist Political Philosophy
completion.thinkerBio: Mary Wollstonecraft (1759-1797) founded the tradition with A Vindication of the Rights of Woman (1792); Susan Moller Okin (1946-2004) at Stanford produced the most influential late twentieth century critique of how mainstream political philosophy ignored gender in Justice, Gender, and the Family (1989).
completion.quote: The family is the first school of justice -- or injustice.

related.name: Liberalism
related.note: Liberal feminism extends the liberal framework to women, but feminist critics show the public/private distinction systematically excludes domestic injustice.

related.name: Justice as Fairness
related.note: Okin's critique applies the Rawlsian veil of ignorance to the family, revealing that gendered domestic arrangements would not survive impartial scrutiny.

related.name: The Second Sex
related.note: De Beauvoir's existentialist analysis of women's condition provides the philosophical foundation for later feminist political critique.

related.name: Reproductive Rights
related.note: Reproductive autonomy is among the central concerns of contemporary feminist political philosophy, grounding bodily integrity as a matter of political equality.

books.title: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
books.author: Mary Wollstonecraft
books.why: The founding text of feminist political philosophy -- the case that women's apparent inferiority results from inadequate education, not nature.

books.title: Justice, Gender, and the Family
books.author: Susan Moller Okin
books.why: Okin's foundational critique applying Rawlsian justice theory to expose the injustice of gendered family structures.

books.title: Women and Human Development
books.author: Martha Nussbaum
books.why: Nussbaum's capabilities approach applied specifically to women's global flourishing and political equality.

completion.name: Colonialism and Postcolonialism
completion.thinkerBio: Frantz Fanon (1925-1961) analyzed the psychological damage of colonization in The Wretched of the Earth (1961); Edward Said (1935-2003) at Columbia founded postcolonial studies as a discipline with Orientalism (1978).
completion.quote: The colonized is elevated above his jungle status in proportion to his adoption of the mother country's cultural standards.

related.name: Negritude
related.note: Negritude is a founding moment of postcolonial thought -- the counter-assertion of colonized cultural value against assimilationist colonial policy.

related.name: Decolonizing Knowledge
related.note: Postcolonial theory grounds the project of decolonizing knowledge -- exposing how Western epistemological frameworks were constructed through colonial power relations.

related.name: Power-Knowledge
related.note: Said's Orientalism applies Foucauldian power-knowledge analysis to show how scholarly representations of the East functioned as instruments of colonial power.

related.name: Pan-Africanism
related.note: Both postcolonial theory and Pan-Africanism address the political and cultural legacies of colonialism, though postcolonialism has a broader global comparative scope.

books.title: The Wretched of the Earth
books.author: Frantz Fanon
books.why: Fanon's foundational analysis of colonial violence, psychological damage, and the path to decolonization.

books.title: Orientalism
books.author: Edward Said
books.why: The founding text of postcolonial studies -- how Western scholarship constructed 'the Orient' as an instrument of colonial power.

books.title: Black Skin, White Masks
books.author: Frantz Fanon
books.why: Fanon's psychoanalytic study of colonial racism's effects on Black subjectivity and identity.

completion.name: Sovereignty
completion.thinkerBio: Jean Bodin (1530-1596) developed the modern concept of sovereignty during the French Wars of Religion in Six Books of the Commonwealth (1576); Carl Schmitt (1888-1985) radicalized it in the twentieth century through his theory of the sovereign as the one who decides on the exception.
completion.quote: Sovereign is he who decides on the exception.

related.name: Social Contract
related.note: Hobbes's social contract theory grounds the necessity of absolute sovereignty as the only alternative to the chaos of the state of nature.

related.name: Biopower
related.note: Foucault explicitly contrasted sovereign power, which decides life and death, with biopower, which administers and optimizes life.

related.name: Biopolitics and Bare Life
related.note: Agamben draws directly on Schmitt's theory of sovereignty to analyze how modern states produce bare life through the state of exception.

related.name: Republicanism
related.note: Republican theory's concern with non-domination challenges Schmittian sovereignty's celebration of decisionistic, potentially arbitrary executive power.

books.title: On Sovereignty
books.author: Jean Bodin
books.why: The foundational early modern text developing the concept of indivisible sovereign authority.

books.title: Political Theology
books.author: Carl Schmitt
books.why: Schmitt's radical twentieth-century theory of sovereignty as the decision on the state of exception.

books.title: Homo Sacer
books.author: Giorgio Agamben
books.why: Agamben's development of Schmittian sovereignty into a theory of bare life and the camp as the paradigm of modern politics.

completion.name: Power and Domination
completion.thinkerBio: Robert Dahl (1915-2014) at Yale developed the pluralist analysis of power as the ability to prevail in observable conflicts; Steven Lukes (b. 1941) developed the influential three-dimensional analysis adding agenda-setting and preference-shaping to overt conflict.
completion.quote: A has power over B to the extent that he can get B to do something B would not otherwise do.

related.name: Hegemony
related.note: Lukes's third dimension of power -- shaping preferences themselves -- converges with Gramsci's account of how hegemony operates through manufactured consent.

related.name: Power-Knowledge
related.note: Foucault's power-knowledge goes further than Lukes -- power is not merely possessed and exercised but is everywhere, productive of subjects and truths.

related.name: Republicanism
related.note: Republican non-domination provides the most precise political analysis of what domination consists in and what institutions are needed to prevent it.

related.name: Civil Disobedience
related.note: Civil disobedience theory analyzes resistance as a response to structural power -- not just individual injustice but systemic domination.

books.title: Power: A Radical View
books.author: Steven Lukes
books.why: The foundational text of the three-dimensional view of power -- overt conflict, agenda-setting, and preference-shaping.

books.title: Who Governs?
books.author: Robert Dahl
books.why: Dahl's pluralist empirical study and theoretical foundation for analyzing power as observable influence over decisions.

books.title: Discipline and Punish
books.author: Michel Foucault
books.why: Foucault's analysis of disciplinary power that goes beyond Lukes's framework to examine power as productive and pervasive.

completion.name: Phenomenology of Perception
completion.thinkerBio: Maurice Merleau-Ponty (1908-1961) wrote Phenomenology of Perception (1945) as his major work, developing a sustained analysis of embodiment that became foundational for both continental philosophy and contemporary cognitive science.
completion.quote: The body is our general medium for having a world.

related.name: Embodied Cognition
related.note: Embodied cognition science draws directly on Merleau-Ponty's account of the body as the pre-reflective vehicle of perception and action.

related.name: Existential Phenomenology
related.note: Phenomenology of Perception is the foundational text of existential phenomenology -- perception understood through lived, embodied existence.

related.name: Substance Dualism
related.note: Merleau-Ponty's account directly challenges the Cartesian picture of the mind as an unextended thinking substance that merely inhabits a body.

related.name: Intersubjectivity
related.note: Merleau-Ponty grounds intersubjectivity in bodily resonance and intercorporeality rather than in theoretical inference about other minds.

books.title: Phenomenology of Perception
books.author: Maurice Merleau-Ponty
books.why: The primary source -- Merleau-Ponty's comprehensive analysis of embodied perception and action.

books.title: Merleau-Ponty's Phenomenology
books.author: Komarine Romdenh-Romluc
books.why: The clearest contemporary scholarly guide to Merleau-Ponty's difficult major work.

books.title: The Visible and the Invisible
books.author: Maurice Merleau-Ponty
books.why: Merleau-Ponty's unfinished late work extending his phenomenology toward a more radical ontology of flesh and reversibility.

completion.name: The Intentionality of Consciousness
completion.thinkerBio: Franz Brentano (1838-1917) revived intentionality from medieval scholasticism as the defining mark of the mental in Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint (1874); Edmund Husserl (1859-1938), his student, made intentionality the foundation of phenomenology.
completion.quote: Every mental phenomenon is characterized by what the Scholastics called the intentional inexistence of an object.

related.name: Cogito Ergo Sum
related.note: Both the cogito and Brentano's intentionality make the first-person perspective philosophically foundational for understanding the mental.

related.name: Chinese Room
related.note: Searle's Chinese Room argument depends on the claim that genuine understanding requires intrinsic intentionality, which mere symbol manipulation cannot supply.

related.name: Phenomenology of Perception
related.note: Husserlian intentionality is the foundation that Merleau-Ponty's existential phenomenology of embodied perception builds upon and transforms.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Naturalizing intentionality in purely functional or causal terms remains one of the deepest challenges for physicalist philosophy of mind.

books.title: Psychology from an Empirical Standpoint
books.author: Franz Brentano
books.why: The foundational text reviving intentionality as the defining mark of the mental.

books.title: Logical Investigations
books.author: Edmund Husserl
books.why: Husserl's foundational development of intentionality into the systematic method of phenomenology.

books.title: Intentionality
books.author: John Searle
books.why: Searle's contemporary analytic treatment of intentionality, connecting it to speech acts and philosophy of mind.

completion.name: Multiple Realizability
completion.thinkerBio: Hilary Putnam (1926-2016) introduced multiple realizability in the late 1960s as the central argument for functionalism, showing that mental states can be realized by radically different physical substrates across different organisms.
completion.quote: The same mental state can be realized in different physical substrates -- human, octopus, or in principle silicon.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Multiple realizability is the central argument for functionalism -- if mental states are realized differently across substrates, they must be defined by function, not physical composition.

related.name: Substance Dualism
related.note: Multiple realizability was developed partly against type-identity theory, a physicalist alternative to dualism that multiple realizability also challenges.

related.name: Reductionism
related.note: Multiple realizability is the central argument against strict psychophysical reduction -- special sciences cannot be reduced if their kinds are multiply realized.

related.name: Philosophy of Biology
related.note: Multiple realizability in philosophy of mind has structural parallels in philosophy of biology's account of convergent evolution producing similar functions through different mechanisms.

books.title: Mind, Language and Reality
books.author: Hilary Putnam
books.why: Putnam's original papers developing multiple realizability and functionalism.

books.title: Philosophy of Mind
books.author: Jaegwon Kim
books.why: The clearest textbook treatment of multiple realizability and its implications for reductionism.

books.title: Representation and Reality
books.author: Hilary Putnam
books.why: Putnam's later reconsideration of the functionalist conclusions he drew from multiple realizability.

completion.name: Consciousness and the Brain
completion.thinkerBio: Francis Crick (1916-2004) and Christof Koch (b. 1956) proposed neural correlates of consciousness research in the 1990s; Stanislas Dehaene (b. 1965) developed Global Workspace Theory into its most empirically rigorous contemporary form.
completion.quote: Consciousness arises when information is broadcast widely across the brain, becoming available to multiple cognitive systems simultaneously.

related.name: Hard Problem of Consciousness
related.note: Neural correlate research addresses the easy problems of consciousness -- Chalmers argues this leaves the hard problem of subjective experience untouched.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Global Workspace Theory is broadly functionalist -- consciousness is defined by the functional role of widespread information broadcasting.

related.name: Attention and Consciousness
related.note: Global Workspace Theory closely connects attention to consciousness -- attention determines what gets broadcast to the global workspace.

related.name: Embodied Cognition
related.note: Global Workspace Theory's brain-centered account contrasts with embodied cognition's emphasis on the whole body and environment in constituting cognition.

books.title: Consciousness and the Brain
books.author: Stanislas Dehaene
books.why: The most rigorous contemporary scientific account of Global Workspace Theory and the neural correlates of consciousness.

books.title: The Astonishing Hypothesis
books.author: Francis Crick
books.why: Crick's foundational case for a purely neuroscientific approach to consciousness.

books.title: The Feeling of What Happens
books.author: Antonio Damasio
books.why: Damasio's complementary neuroscientific account connecting consciousness to bodily emotion and feeling.

completion.name: Animal Consciousness
completion.thinkerBio: Donald Griffin (1915-2003) founded cognitive ethology, documenting sophisticated animal cognition; the Cambridge Declaration on Consciousness (2012), signed by leading neuroscientists, formally recognized non-human consciousness as scientifically established.
completion.quote: The neurological substrates of consciousness are not unique to humans -- non-human animals also possess these neurological substrates.

related.name: Qualia
related.note: Nagel's question of what it is like to be a bat directly raises the issue of animal consciousness and its irreducibly subjective character.

related.name: Theory of Mind
related.note: Whether non-human animals have theory of mind is closely connected to debates about animal consciousness and cognitive sophistication.

related.name: Environmental Ethics
related.note: Animal consciousness has direct ethical implications -- if animals have genuine experience, their welfare must figure in moral calculations.

related.name: Buddhist Ethics
related.note: Buddhist ethics already extends moral concern to all sentient beings, anticipating the contemporary scientific recognition of animal consciousness.

books.title: Animal Minds
books.author: Donald Griffin
books.why: Griffin's foundational work establishing cognitive ethology and the scientific study of animal minds.

books.title: Are We Smart Enough to Know How Smart Animals Are?
books.author: Frans de Waal
books.why: De Waal's accessible survey of contemporary research on animal cognition and consciousness.

books.title: Animal Liberation
books.author: Peter Singer
books.why: Singer's foundational ethical argument that animal sentience grounds direct moral obligations.

completion.name: Artificial Intelligence and Mind
completion.thinkerBio: Alan Turing (1912-1954) proposed the imitation game as an operational test for machine intelligence in 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence' (1950); John Searle's Chinese Room argument (1980) remains the most influential philosophical challenge to claims of genuine machine understanding.
completion.quote: Can machines think? The question is, I believe, too meaningless to deserve discussion -- but we can ask whether machines can pass the imitation game.

related.name: Chinese Room
related.note: The Chinese Room remains the central philosophical challenge to claims that computational systems genuinely understand rather than merely simulate understanding.

related.name: Functionalism
related.note: Functionalism is the philosophical foundation for strong AI -- if minds are functional organizations, they can in principle be implemented in silicon.

related.name: The Turing Test
related.note: Turing's behavioral test for machine intelligence remains the most famous, if contested, operational criterion in the field.

related.name: Hard Problem of Consciousness
related.note: Whether AI systems could be genuinely conscious, not merely behaviorally sophisticated, connects directly to the hard problem of consciousness.

books.title: The Essential Turing
books.author: Alan Turing (ed. B. Jack Copeland)
books.why: Contains Turing's foundational paper proposing the imitation game as a test for machine intelligence.

books.title: Minds, Brains and Science
books.author: John Searle
books.why: Searle's accessible development of the Chinese Room argument and its implications for strong AI.

books.title: Superintelligence
books.author: Nick Bostrom
books.why: Bostrom's analysis of the philosophical and existential stakes of advanced artificial intelligence.

completion.name: The Tao
completion.thinkerBio: Laozi (traditional dates: sixth century BCE) is the traditional author of the Tao Te Ching; Zhuangzi (c. 369-286 BCE) developed the philosophy of the Tao with greater literary and argumentative sophistication in the text bearing his name.
completion.quote: The Tao that can be told is not the eternal Tao.

related.name: Wu Wei
related.note: Wu wei is the practical expression of the Tao -- acting in alignment with the natural flow rather than imposing human will against it.

related.name: Yin-Yang
related.note: Yin and yang are the complementary forces through which the Tao's dynamic structure manifests in all phenomena.

related.name: Process Philosophy
related.note: Both the Tao and Whitehead's process philosophy treat reality as fundamentally dynamic and relational rather than composed of static substances.

related.name: The Concept of De
related.note: De is the particular expression of the Tao in each individual thing -- the Tao is universal, de is its specific manifestation.

books.title: Tao Te Ching
books.author: Laozi (trans. Ursula K. Le Guin)
books.why: Le Guin's literary translation -- the most beautiful and philosophically resonant English version.

books.title: The Complete Works of Zhuangzi
books.author: Zhuangzi (trans. Burton Watson)
books.why: Zhuangzi's profound and often playful philosophical elaboration of the Tao.

books.title: Tao: The Watercourse Way
books.author: Alan Watts
books.why: Watts's accessible philosophical introduction connecting the Tao to broader themes in comparative philosophy.

completion.name: Dependent Origination
completion.thinkerBio: The historical Buddha (c. 480-400 BCE) taught dependent origination as the causal structure underlying suffering and its cessation; Nagarjuna (c. 150-250 CE) gave it its most rigorous philosophical development through the doctrine of emptiness in Madhyamaka philosophy.
completion.quote: When this exists, that comes to be; with the arising of this, that arises. When this does not exist, that does not come to be.

related.name: Anatta
related.note: Anatta is explained by dependent origination -- there is no self because everything arises dependently, including what we call 'I.'

related.name: Dukkha
related.note: The twelve links of dependent origination describe precisely how dukkha perpetuates itself through craving, clinging, and becoming.

related.name: Madhyamaka Philosophy
related.note: Nagarjuna radicalized dependent origination into a complete metaphysics of emptiness -- nothing has intrinsic existence apart from its conditions.

related.name: Causation
related.note: Buddhist dependent origination and Hume's analysis of causation both deny that things possess intrinsic causal powers independent of their relational conditions, though from very different starting points.

books.title: What the Buddha Taught
books.author: Walpola Rahula
books.why: The clearest accessible explanation of dependent origination and its place in Buddhist philosophy.

books.title: Nagarjuna's Middle Way
books.author: Nagarjuna (trans. Mark Siderits and Shoryu Katsura)
books.why: A rigorous scholarly translation of Nagarjuna's foundational philosophical text on emptiness and dependent origination.

books.title: Buddhism as Philosophy
books.author: Mark Siderits
books.why: A clear analytic philosophical introduction to Buddhist metaphysics including dependent origination.

completion.name: Confucian Li
completion.thinkerBio: Confucius (551-479 BCE) developed li (ritual propriety) as central to moral cultivation in the Analects; Xunzi (c. 310-235 BCE) gave li a different philosophical foundation, treating it as the civilizing constraint required by an inherently selfish human nature.
completion.quote: Through ritual propriety, one becomes fully human.

related.name: Confucian Ren
related.note: Li is the external form through which ren is expressed -- and through whose sincere practice ren is cultivated in return.

related.name: Mencius on Human Nature
related.note: Mencius holds human nature is fundamentally good, requiring li only to nurture inherent virtue; Xunzi holds li is needed to constrain inherently selfish human nature.

related.name: Speech Act Theory
related.note: Confucian li and Austin's speech act theory both emphasize that performing the correct social forms is itself a meaningful and constitutive act, not mere convention.

related.name: Virtue Ethics
related.note: Both Confucian li and Aristotelian virtue ethics hold that habituated practice of correct external forms cultivates internal moral character over time.

books.title: The Analects
books.author: Confucius (trans. Edward Slingerland)
books.why: The primary source for li's role in Confucian moral cultivation.

books.title: Xunzi: The Complete Text
books.author: Xunzi (trans. Eric Hutton)
books.why: The most important alternative Confucian account of li, grounded in a skeptical view of innate human nature.

books.title: Confucius and the Analects
books.author: Bryan Van Norden (ed.)
books.why: Scholarly essays covering the full range of interpretations of li and its relationship to ren.

completion.name: Yin-Yang
completion.thinkerBio: Yin-yang cosmology developed across the Warring States period (475-221 BCE) and was systematized in texts including the Yijing and the work of the Yin-Yang school associated with Zou Yan (305-240 BCE).
completion.quote: Yin and yang are the root of all transformation -- each contains the seed of the other.

related.name: The Tao
related.note: Yin and yang are the complementary forces through which the Tao's dynamic structure manifests in all phenomena and processes.

related.name: The Yijing and Correlative Thought
related.note: The Yijing's hexagram system is built directly on the interplay of yin and yang lines representing dynamic configurations of cosmic process.

related.name: Process Philosophy
related.note: Both yin-yang cosmology and Whiteheadian process philosophy treat reality as fundamentally dynamic and relational, constituted by ongoing transformation.

related.name: Dialectics
related.note: Hegelian dialectics resolves contradiction through synthesis and sublation; yin-yang holds opposites in permanent dynamic balance without seeking final resolution.

books.title: The Yijing
books.author: trans. Richard John Lynn
books.why: A scholarly translation of the foundational text systematizing yin-yang correlative cosmology.

books.title: The Way of Heaven
books.author: Robin Wang
books.why: A rigorous philosophical analysis of yin-yang thought across the Chinese philosophical tradition.

books.title: The Path of Chinese Philosophy
books.author: Bryan Van Norden
books.why: An accessible comprehensive introduction situating yin-yang within the broader landscape of Chinese philosophy.

completion.name: Brahman and Atman
completion.thinkerBio: The doctrine of Brahman and Atman is developed across the Upanishads (c. 800-200 BCE) and given its most systematic philosophical articulation by Adi Shankaracharya (c. 788-820 CE) in his Advaita Vedanta commentaries.
completion.quote: Tat tvam asi -- That thou art.

related.name: Advaita Vedanta
related.note: Advaita Vedanta is the systematic philosophical development of the Upanishadic identity of Atman and Brahman into a complete metaphysical system.

related.name: Moksha
related.note: The direct realization of the identity of Atman and Brahman constitutes moksha -- liberation from the cycle of rebirth.

related.name: Panpsychism
related.note: Both the Brahman-Atman doctrine and panpsychism make consciousness fundamental to all reality, though through very different metaphysical frameworks.

related.name: Idealism
related.note: The non-dual identity of Atman and Brahman is structurally similar to certain forms of Western idealism that identify ultimate reality with mind or consciousness.

books.title: The Upanishads
books.author: trans. Eknath Easwaran
books.why: An accessible and philosophically attentive translation of the primary source texts on Brahman and Atman.

books.title: The Crest-Jewel of Discrimination
books.author: Shankaracharya (trans. Swami Prabhavananda)
books.why: Shankara's systematic philosophical treatment of the identity between Atman and Brahman.

books.title: Indian Philosophy: A Very Short Introduction
books.author: Sue Hamilton
books.why: A clear introduction situating the Brahman-Atman doctrine within the broader landscape of Indian philosophy.

completion.name: Moksha
completion.thinkerBio: Moksha is discussed across all major schools of Hindu philosophy; the Bhagavad Gita synthesizes the major paths (jnana, bhakti, karma yoga) toward it, while Patanjali's Yoga Sutras provide the most systematic practical methodology.
completion.quote: Liberation is not the acquisition of something new but the removal of the ignorance that obscures what was already true.

related.name: Brahman and Atman
related.note: Moksha consists in the direct realization of the identity of Atman and Brahman, dissolving the ignorance that generates the illusion of separate selfhood.

related.name: Karma
related.note: Moksha is the cessation of the conditions -- accumulated karma driven by ignorance and desire -- that perpetuate the cycle of rebirth.

related.name: Yoga Philosophy
related.note: Patanjali's eightfold yogic path is the systematic practical methodology for achieving moksha through the stilling of mental fluctuations.

related.name: Nirvana
related.note: Both moksha and Buddhist nirvana name a state of liberation from suffering and the cycle of rebirth, though they differ in their underlying metaphysics of self.

books.title: The Bhagavad Gita
books.author: trans. Barbara Stoler Miller
books.why: The Gita's synthesis of the major paths to moksha -- knowledge, devotion, and selfless action.

books.title: The Yoga Sutras of Patanjali
books.author: trans. Edwin Bryant
books.why: The systematic practical methodology for achieving moksha through yogic discipline.

books.title: Classical Indian Philosophy
books.author: Bina Gupta
books.why: A comprehensive scholarly survey of moksha across the major schools of Indian philosophy.

completion.name: Buddhist Ethics
completion.thinkerBio: Buddhist ethics is grounded in the Pali Canon's teachings attributed to the historical Buddha; the Mahayana bodhisattva ideal, developed by figures including Shantideva (eighth century CE), extends ethical concern to the liberation of all sentient beings.
completion.quote: Hatred is never appeased by hatred in this world. By non-hatred alone is hatred appeased.

related.name: Karma
related.note: Karma is the causal mechanism through which ethical action bears fruit -- understanding it is fundamental to Buddhist ethical motivation.

related.name: Dukkha
related.note: Buddhist ethics is organized around the goal of liberation from dukkha, cultivating wisdom and compassion as means to that liberation.

related.name: The Mahayana Bodhisattva Ideal
related.note: The bodhisattva ideal extends Buddhist ethical concern from personal liberation to universal compassion for all sentient beings.

related.name: Virtue Ethics
related.note: Both Buddhist ethics and Aristotelian virtue ethics center moral life on the cultivation of stable character dispositions rather than rule-following alone.

books.title: The Dhammapada
books.author: trans. Eknath Easwaran
books.why: The most accessible canonical source for the core ethical teachings of Buddhism.

books.title: A Guide to the Bodhisattva's Way of Life
books.author: Shantideva (trans. Vesna Wallace and Alan Wallace)
books.why: Shantideva's philosophically sophisticated development of universal compassion and the bodhisattva ideal.

books.title: Buddhist Ethics: A Very Short Introduction
books.author: Damien Keown
books.why: A clear, comprehensive introduction to the structure and content of Buddhist ethical thought.

completion.name: The Bhagavad Gita and Action
completion.thinkerBio: The Bhagavad Gita, embedded in the epic Mahabharata, is traditionally attributed to the sage Vyasa and dates to roughly the second century BCE to second century CE; it remains the single most influential text in Hindu philosophy and practice.
completion.quote: You have the right to perform your prescribed duties, but you are not entitled to the fruits of your actions.

related.name: Dharma
related.note: The Gita's central teaching is fulfilling one's dharma without attachment to results -- nishkama karma is the ideal of dharmic action.

related.name: Karma
related.note: The Gita develops the most philosophically sophisticated treatment of karma through the doctrine of action performed without attachment to outcomes.

related.name: Stoic Ethics
related.note: Both the Gita's nishkama karma and Stoic ethics counsel performing one's duty fully while remaining detached from outcomes outside one's control.

related.name: Moksha
related.note: The Gita synthesizes the major paths to moksha -- knowledge, devotion, and selfless action -- as complementary rather than competing routes.

books.title: The Bhagavad Gita
books.author: trans. Barbara Stoler Miller
books.why: The primary source -- an elegant and philosophically attentive translation of the foundational text.

books.title: The Bhagavad Gita: A New Translation
books.author: trans. Stephen Mitchell
books.why: A literary translation with an accessible introduction connecting the Gita's themes to contemporary spiritual concerns.

books.title: The Bhagavad Gita for Daily Living
books.author: Eknath Easwaran
books.why: A practical commentary connecting the Gita's philosophy of action to everyday ethical life.

completion.name: Formalism in Art
completion.thinkerBio: Clive Bell (1881-1964) introduced 'significant form' as the criterion of aesthetic value in Art (1914); Roger Fry (1866-1934) developed a parallel formalist account of post-impressionist painting, together founding the Bloomsbury aesthetic tradition.
completion.quote: Significant form is that arrangement and combination of lines and colours that moves us aesthetically, apart from any representational content.

related.name: Aesthetic Disinterestedness
related.note: Aesthetic formalism inherits Kantian disinterestedness -- pure formal appreciation requires suspension of all practical and representational interests.

related.name: Mimesis
related.note: Formalism explicitly rejects the idea that representational accuracy or content has aesthetic value independent of formal arrangement.

related.name: Art as Expression
related.note: Formalism locates aesthetic value purely in formal properties; expression theory locates it in the externalization of the artist's emotional experience.

related.name: The Ontology of Art
related.note: Formalism's emphasis on structure connects to questions about what precisely constitutes the identity of an artwork across different media and instances.

books.title: Art
books.author: Clive Bell
